nativeRange.habitat: Sluggish or still waters across the Ganges, Brahmaputra, and Irrawaddy basins, including floodplains, ponds, oxbow lakes, swamps, wetlands, and rice paddies. The species favors heavily vegetated margins with leaf litter along slow-moving or standing sections of rivers and associated lowland habitats.

decorPreferences: dense planting (Amazon swords, Java fern, Vallisneria) for cover and ambush sites
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, dried oak leaves) to replicate natural biotope and lower pH
driftwood and twisted roots for hiding and ambush staging
caves or PVC pipes as retreat areas
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce surface brightness
dark substrate (fine sand or fine gravel)

diet.notes: In practice, the Nandus feeds almost exclusively on live fish and large invertebrates, mirroring its wild diet. In captivity it nearly always refuses prepared and frozen foods; live feeder fish such as guppies and mosquitofish are typically the only reliably accepted food. Some individuals may eventually take live earthworms or large live shrimp with persistent conditioning, but a dependable transition to non-live foods is rare. A consistent supply of appropriately sized live feeder fish is therefore essential. Juveniles can be started on live bloodworms and small live fish.

goodWith: large, robust, fast-moving fish too big to swallow (as tankmates, not prey) — with caution
large barbs or similarly sized fish in very spacious tanks — with caution
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (effectively any fish under ~10 cm)
small tetras, rasboras, and nano fish
small livebearers (guppies, endlers — used as feeders only)
conspecifics unless in very large tanks with multiple hiding zones
other ambush predators of similar size

imageCandidates.notes: Beta Mahatvaraj; 5472x3648. Only live in-aquarium specimen found: sharp dark-background shot showing the diagnostic mottled leaf-mimic camouflage, spiny dorsal and protrusible mouth on a vibrant live fish. Caveat: bears a corner signature/logo watermark that does not obscure the fish.
Shino jacob koottanad; 4199x3146. Sharp in-hand freshly-caught specimen, whole fish in frame, clearly showing gold-brown mottled cryptic pattern, dark blotches and spiny dorsal.
Divya Datta; 3576x1485. Sharp clean lateral view on white background showing body shape, mottled markings and orange-tinged fins; complements the other angles.

summary: The Nandus, also known as the Gangetic Leaf Fish, is an Asian ambush predator that can reach around 20 cm. It mimics dead leaves to stalk prey, then strikes with highly expandable jaws capable of engulfing fish nearly its own size. In captivity it almost always refuses prepared or frozen foods and requires a steady supply of live feeder fish.
careNotes:
Nandus nandus is the Asian equivalent of the South American leaf fish (Monocirrhus polyacanthus), using cryptic mottled brown coloration and a motionless, head-down posture to mimic dead leaves before striking prey with explosive speed. Its protrusible jaws can expand to engulf fish approaching its own body length. Adult size of 18–20 cm total length is routinely reported in wild specimens, with aquarium fish typically reaching 15–18 cm.

The paramount challenge is feeding. The vast majority of specimens will accept only live fish in captivity and very rarely transition to frozen or prepared foods despite extended conditioning attempts. A reliable, sustainable supply of live feeder fish (guppies, mosquitofish, rosy red minnows) is not optional — it is a non-negotiable requirement. Some keepers report occasional acceptance of large live earthworms or live river shrimp as dietary variety, but these should be viewed as supplements rather than substitutes.

A single-specimen setup is the standard recommendation. Conspecific aggression is intense and territorial disputes in confined spaces result in serious injury or death. Housing with other fish is viable only in very large tanks (300+ L) with robust fish too large to swallow, though the Nandus will still ambush anything that fits in its mouth.

Water conditions should lean toward the slightly acidic and soft side of its tolerance range, reflecting its native floodplain and wetland biotope. Indian almond leaves and driftwood help establish appropriate chemistry and provide the leaf-litter aesthetic that encourages natural behavior. A well-covered tank is important as this species can jump.

See also: [south-american-leaf-fish] — the ecological equivalent from South America with very similar demands.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely accomplished and poorly documented in the hobby. Wild spawning occurs during the monsoon season when floodplains inundate. Males are reported to guard eggs deposited on flat surfaces or among vegetation. Conditioning requires consistent live-food supply and soft, slightly acidic water. A species-only breeding setup with minimal disturbance is required. Captive breeding remains a specialist achievement.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nandus nandus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 20 cm), temperature range, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness, native range across South Asia (India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, Pakistan, Myanmar), natural habitat (floodplains, ponds, wetlands, rice paddies), diet (obligate live feeder), feeding difficulty, conspecific aggression, tank requirements, and breeding notes. Confirmed Nandidae family, Perciformes order.

additional.name: FishBase — Nandus nandus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max reported 20 cm TL), temperature range (22–28°C), distribution map confirming Ganges–Brahmaputra–Irrawaddy basin presence across India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, Myanmar, and Pakistan. Confirmed carnivore diet classification. Taxonomy: Nandidae, Perciformes. IUCN status not formally assessed.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Nandus nandus profile
additional.notes: Confirmed live-feeder dependency, cryptic leaf-mimic behavior, protrusible jaw description, and specialist-only care classification. Noted that transition to non-live foods is very rarely achieved. Tank size and decor recommendations (heavy planting, leaf litter, dim lighting) corroborated. Conspecific aggression and single-specimen recommendation confirmed.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Species Research Notes (Nandus / Asian Leaf Fish)
additional.notes: Aquarium Co-Op does not maintain a dedicated Nandus nandus species page. General predatory fish husbandry guidance on live-feeder dependency, tank sizing for ambush predators, and species-only setups informed general care framing. Cross-referenced with SeriouslyFish and FishBase for all specific parameters.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Asian Leaf Fishes of the Family Nandidae
additional.notes: TFH historical coverage of Nandidae family husbandry confirmed ambush-predator behavior, explosive protrusible jaw strike mechanics, near-total live-feeder reliance, parallels to Monocirrhus (South American leaf fish), mottled cryptic coloration as leaf mimicry, and specialist-only suitability. Corroborated 20 cm adult size and South Asian distribution.

fish.breedingNotes: The Nandus is rarely bred in captivity and represents a specialist achievement when it does occur. A species-only setup is recommended, with consistent live-food conditioning, soft slightly acidic water, and minimal disturbance throughout the breeding period. In the wild, spawning is triggered by the monsoon season; replicating water-level fluctuation or rain-simulation may help induce breeding in captivity. Males have been reported to guard egg clutches. Fry require live micro-foods from the outset — infusoria and baby brine shrimp nauplii initially, progressing to small live fish as they grow.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: This species almost never accepts prepared or frozen foods and requires a reliable supply of live feeder fish. It is typically kept alone due to intense conspecific aggression and will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow. Adults reach around 20 cm and present a long-term live-food commitment that places this species in the specialist category.
